Band inversion is necessary for band warping in TIs; however, it is not sufficient. There are several examples of TIs that, despite being chemically similar, possess different band structure shapes (warped vs. non-warped) and, therefore, different valley degeneracies. A notable pairing is Bi 2 Se 3 and Bi 2 Te 3. While both TIs possess inverted bands at the Γ-point, the bands in Bi 2 Se 3 are centered at the Γ-point and therefore not warped, resulting in a valley degeneracy of 1 ([Figure 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#fig3)B). On the other hand, the bands are warped at the Γ-point in Bi 2 Te 3, moving the band edges to _k_-points on the bisectrix plane of the Brillouin zone.[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#bib39) This results in a valley degeneracy of 6 ([Figure 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#fig3)C). In alloys of Bi 2 Te 3, there is a possibility that multiple warped bands contribute to an even higher valley degeneracy of up to 12,[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#bib40) as suggested by ARPES measurements.[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#bib41) Additionally, in IV–VI rock-salt TIs, band inversion occurs at the L-point.[42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#bib42) While the bands in SnTe are centered at the L-point and not warped so that the valley degeneracy is 4 ([Figure 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#fig3)D), bands in the metastable rock-salt phase of SnSe are warped so that the band edges are offset from the L-point, giving rise to a valley degeneracy of 24 ([Figure 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#fig3)E).

Recently, a k·p perturbation theory-based band structure model was used to derive a mathematical condition for when inverted bands become warped in TIs that are centrosymmetric and obey time-reversal symmetry (e.g., non-magnetic TIs).[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#bib38) The model elucidated the importance of the _band inversion strength_ of a TI, M 0, where its magnitude is defined as(Equation 1)|M 0|=E CB(k 0)−E VB(k 0)2,where E CB and E VB are the conduction and valence band edges, respectively, and k 0 is the k-point where band inversion occurs. In other words, |M 0| is a measure of the energy separation between the lowest unoccupied conduction band and the highest occupied valence band at k 0. For a TI, the sign of M 0 is negative, and more negative values correspond to stronger band inversion. Note that normal insulators with non-inverted bands can also be represented using [Equation 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#fd1) but with a positive M 0.

The k·p model showed mathematically that, when M 0 is sufficiently negative and past a certain threshold value, the bands in a TI become warped. Physically, this means that the bands must be sufficiently inverted for warping to occur in a TI ([Figure 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#fig3)A). A more thorough derivation and discussion of the mathematical condition can be found in Toriyama and Snyder.[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#bib38),[43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#bib43) This band inversion-driven warping phenomenon can explain the distinct band structure shapes between Bi 2 Se 3 and Bi 2 Te 3. Indeed, the energy separation between the conduction and valence bands at the Γ-point is larger in Bi 2 Te 3 (M 0= −0.23 eV in [Figure 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#fig3)C) than in Bi 2 Se 3 (M 0= −0.12 eV in [Figure 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#fig3)B), thereby explaining the warped bands in Bi 2 Te 3 and the non-warped bands in Bi 2 Se 3. Because the band inversion strength M 0 is a characteristic of the band structure, it is principally modulated by (1) spin-orbit coupling (SOC)[44](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#bib44),[45](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#bib45),[46](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#bib46) and (2) atomic orbital interactions (e.g., sp-mixing).[47](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#bib47),[48](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#bib48) Therefore, the stronger band inversion (i.e., more negative M 0) in Bi 2 Te 3, as well as the band warping in Bi 2 Te 3, can be attributed to the stronger SOC. Furthermore, the band inversion is stronger at the L-point in cubic SnSe (M 0= −0.28 eV in [Figure 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#fig3)E) than in SnTe (M 0= −0.05 eV in [Figure 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666675824002200#fig3)D), providing further credence to th

enriched_points.expanded_content: Topological insulators (TIs) are poised to enhance commercial technologies, especially in quantum computing, where their unique surface states could lead to faster and more efficient processing.
Research on TIs is crucial for quantum computing advancements, as they offer fault-tolerant qubits and robust edge states, potentially revolutionizing information processing technologies.
TIs facilitate spintronic applications by enabling spin-polarized currents, which can lead to low-power, high-speed electronic devices, making them attractive for future technologies.
The unique properties of TIs enhance photonic technologies through robust edge states, allowing for new optical devices that are resistant to backscattering and disorder.
TIs are being explored for thermoelectric applications, as their surface states can provide high efficiency in converting temperature differences into electrical energy.
Artificial intelligence is accelerating the discovery of novel TIs, optimizing material properties for commercial applications, and enhancing the understanding of complex topological phases.
Research aims to achieve room-temperature operational stability in TIs, which is critical for their practical applications in consumer electronics and computing devices.
Scaling TIs for widespread use faces challenges, including material quality, defect management, and integration with existing semiconductor technologies.
